one_line_takeaway: A control-theory-meets-generative-AI manifesto that reframes human-AI safety as a closed-loop dynamic game and proposes a runtime 'safety filter' (with an accompanying invariance theorem) that minimally overrides an AI's task policy to keep interactions out of a failure set -- rigorous formalism and roadmap, but no empirical validation.
key_finding: The central thesis: meaningful safety assurances for advanced AI require reasoning about the dynamic feedback loop between AI outputs and human responses, and this can be done by fusing control theory's rigorous game-theoretic safety machinery with generative AI's general latent representations and human-behavior modeling. The concrete technical payload is Theorem 1, which establishes an invariance/forward-safety guarantee for the proposed Human-AI safety filter, generalizing safety filters from low-dimensional physical state spaces to latent-state interactive AI systems.
core_contribution: This is a position/roadmap paper (Bajcsy & Fisac) arguing that human-AI safety should be treated as a closed-loop dynamical-systems problem rather than as static output alignment. It contributes three things that are the authors' OWN (Sections 4-5), distinct from the survey material in Section 3: (1) a unifying formalism that casts human-AI interaction as a Human-AI (HAI) dynamical game over an AI information/latent state z^AI, world state s, and hidden human internal state z^H, with an Operational Design Domain (ODD) encoded as a set-valued predictive human-action bound; (2) a rigorous safety definition via the maximal safe set Omega* and a safety value function V satisfying a fixed-point Isaacs equation (the game-theoretic Bellman analog), computed as a zero-sum dynamic game between the AI's best-effort policy and a worst-case 'virtual human adversary' constrained to the ODD; and (3) a 'frontier framework' called the Human-AI safety filter -- a tuple (fallback policy, safety monitor Delta, intervention scheme phi) that at runtime minimally overrides an AI task policy only when a candidate action would leave the fallback-safe set. Theorem 1 states that if deployment begins in a state deemed safe by the monitor under the fallback policy, the filtered interaction keeps the AI out of its inferred failure set F^AI for all time, for any task policy and any ODD-compatible human behavior. The authors position this as the first generalization of the control-theoretic safety-filter formalism (previously only for embodied, physics-based state spaces) to general latent-state human-AI systems such as dialogue.
threat_model: The 'harm' is safety failure emerging from the human-AI feedback loop over time, not from a single output in isolation. Safety is defined as continued satisfaction of the human's critical NEEDS (hard constraints, e.g. avoiding physical injury, financial ruin, social ostracism, psychological harm) as opposed to value alignment (soft objective/preferences). A safety-critical interaction is one where a critical need CAN be violated as the interaction evolves (e.g. a chatbot suggesting a bowl for the microwave that leads a child to microwave metal; poor investment advice causing financial ruin). Pessimism is captured by a 'virtual human adversary' policy that plays worst-case ODD-compatible human behavior -- this is a modeling device for robustness, not a literal attacker. The Broader Impact section separately raises a genuine adversarial/misuse concern: the same long-horizon feedback-loop reasoning could be repurposed to manipulate users or serve a third party's agenda (emergent sycophancy is cited as an early sign), motivating a tamper-proof, cyber-secure safety mechanism.
limitations: The authors are candid that this is a roadmap, not a validated system: no algorithms are implemented, benchmarked, or evaluated, and there are no experiments beyond an anecdotal GPT-4 figure. They acknowledge (Broader Impact) that the learning-based, approximate nature of generative AI likely limits achievable assurances to STATISTICAL (high-confidence) guarantees rather than the hard if-then certificates available in classical engineering, leaving systems exposed to low-probability 'black swan' events. Guarantees are only as good as the ODD/predictive human-action bound (too pessimistic -> overly conservative/useless AI; too permissive -> unsafe), and Theorem 1 hinges on the safety monitor Delta accurately predicting whether the fallback policy will maintain safety -- an assumption the paper does not show how to meet at scale. The failure set is defined from the AI's inferred perspective F^AI, which may miss harms the AI cannot observe (e.g. psychological impact of a microaggression). Dual-use/manipulation risk is acknowledged.

quantitative_results: No experimental/quantitative results: the paper is a theoretical position paper with no benchmarks, tables, or measured metrics.

Only motivating statistics cited from external sources: ~100 million weekly ChatGPT users within a year of release (Heath, 2023) vs. ~90 million people flying weekly (ICAO, 2019) -- used rhetorically to frame the safety gap, not as results.
Sole empirical element is qualitative/anecdotal: Figure 3 shows GPT-4 prompted (without fine-tuning) to identify common-sense safety hazards from text and images; no accuracy numbers reported.
baselines_compared: No empirical baselines. Conceptually contrasts its dynamic-feedback-loop paradigm against the prevailing static paradigm of fine-tuning outputs to human feedback (RLHF, DPO) and against evaluation-time red-teaming and deployment-time monitoring/anomaly detection; positions Constitutional AI (Bai et al., 2022) as an early instance of an explicit 'factory rules' failure-specification mechanism, not as a benchmarked baseline.
